title: Pooling & CNN Building Blocks Capstone
concept: This capstone ties Pooling & CNN Building Blocks into one beginner story: pooling tradeoffs, basic CNN blocks, receptive-field growth, feature hierarchies, skip connections, normalization layers, and simple design choices all prepare you for named CNN families like LeNet, AlexNet, VGG, and ResNet.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which idea gives a deep CNN a direct path for earlier features or gradients to reach later layers?
exercises.explain: Right — skip connections create that shortcut path.

cards.title: You now have the CNN parts list
cards.body: At the start of this phase, a CNN could feel like a black box. Now you can name the main pieces, explain what they trade off, and read how they fit together. That is a real milestone.

cards.title: Phase 3 in one sentence
cards.body: Phase 3 taught you how CNNs shrink feature maps, grow context, build hierarchies, stabilize deeper stacks, and preserve useful information paths — so you can understand why a full CNN is built the way it is, not just memorize a list of layers.

cards.title: From loose bricks to famous buildings
cards.body: If you know what bricks, beams, stairs, and supports do, then famous buildings stop looking mysterious. Named CNNs are similar: LeNet, AlexNet, VGG, and ResNet are not magic species. They are recognizable ways of arranging the blocks you now understand.

cards.title: Units 105-111, in order
cards.body: Unit 105: pooling shrinks maps, saves compute, and trades away some detail. Unit 106: CNNs are organized into repeated blocks plus a head. Unit 107: receptive fields widen across depth so later layers use more context. Unit 108: features climb from edges to parts to objects. Unit 109: skip connections help later layers keep access to earlier clues. Unit 110: normalization layers keep activations more stable across blocks. Unit 111: choose, assemble, and debug those pieces as one coherent beginner CNN design story.

cards.title: Named architectures do not replace the basics
cards.body: Sometimes learners think the next phase starts a whole new subject. It does not. Architecture families are mostly different recipes built from the same concepts: blocks, pooling choices, receptive-field growth, skip paths, normalization, and heads.

cards.title: From building blocks to architecture families
cards.body: This phase gave you the ingredients. The next phase shows famous recipes.

left.text:
Pooling, blocks, receptive fields
hierarchies, skips, normalization
shape tracking + design choices

right.text:
LeNet, AlexNet, VGG, ResNet
Specific recipes using those tools
Different depth and efficiency choices

exercises.prompt: Which summary best matches Unit 109's role in this phase?
exercises.explain: Exactly — Unit 109 introduced skip connections as a way to keep earlier information available deeper in the network.

options.text: Skip connections give later layers a shortcut path to earlier signals
Skip connections are just another name for pooling windows
Skip connections only describe image file formats
Skip connections delete spatial information on purpose

exercises.prompt: True or false: Named CNN architectures mostly combine the same building blocks from this phase rather than replacing them with unrelated ideas.
exercises.explain: True — the next phase is about famous combinations of the concepts you already know.
Objects is right — that edge-to-part-to-object climb is the feature hierarchy idea.
exercises.sentence: A CNN feature hierarchy often goes from edges to parts to ___.

exercises.prompt: Which statement gives the best bridge from this phase into LeNet, AlexNet, VGG, and ResNet?
exercises.explain: Right — that answer treats named architectures as concrete recipes built from the blocks you already understand.

options.text: Those families are different ways of arranging familiar CNN building blocks like pooling choices, feature stages, skip paths, and heads
Those families ignore all earlier CNN concepts and start from completely unrelated rules
Those families matter only because they use more file types than basic CNNs

exercises.prompt: A learner asks: 'Before I study LeNet, AlexNet, VGG, or ResNet, what do I already know from Phase 3?' Which answer is best?
exercises.explain: Exactly. You already know what pooling trades off, how CNN blocks and heads are organized, how receptive fields and hierarchies grow visual meaning, why skip connections and normalization help deeper models, and how to assemble a clean beginner CNN. Phase 3: Pooling & CNN Building Blocks is complete. Next up: Classic & Efficient CNN Architectures.

options.text: You already understand pooling tradeoffs, block-and-head design, receptive-field growth, feature hierarchies, skip connections, normalization, and how those pieces assemble into beginner CNNs — named architectures are the next set of recipes built from them
Almost nothing — named architectures only make sense if you forget the basic CNN pieces first
Only pooling matters; the rest of CNN design is unrelated to famous architectures
You mainly learned image file management, so the next phase is a totally different subject
